content:
# Disagreements — Genesis 41 (GEN_041)

## 1. Shnatayim Yamim — "Two Full Years" vs. "Two Years of Days" (v. 1)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "two years of days" | Preserves the Hebraism shnatayim yamim |
| LOGOS | "two full years" | yamim is an appositional intensifier = "full" |
| PNEUMA | "two full years" | Same as LOGOS |
| **SOPHIA** | **"two full years" — 2-1** | The yamim intensifier emphasizes completeness; "two years of days" is awkward in English without theological necessity. "Two full years" captures the emphasis naturally. |

## 2. Min-ha-Bor — "Pit" vs. "Dungeon" (v. 14)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "from the pit" | Preserves the bor verbal thread from ch. 37 |
| LOGOS | "from the pit" | Same — crucial lexical link |
| PNEUMA | "out of the dungeon" | Contextually accurate dynamic rendering |
| **SOPHIA** | **"from the pit" — 2-1** | The bor thread (37:20-24 → 40:15 → 41:14) is one of the most significant lexical links in the Joseph cycle. "Dungeon" is contextually valid but breaks this thread. The same word must describe all three of Joseph's imprisonments. |

## 3. Shelom Par'oh — "Peace" vs. "Welfare" vs. "What Is Good for Him" (v. 16)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Pharaoh's peace" | shalom → peace |
| LOGOS | "Pharaoh's welfare" | shalom in practical context = welfare/well-being |
| PNEUMA | "Pharaoh with what is good for him" | Dynamic rendering of shalom |
| **SOPHIA** | **"Pharaoh's welfare" — LOGOS upheld** | In this administrative context, shalom means total well-being and good outcome, not the absence of conflict. "Peace" misleads in modern English. "What is good for him" is too free. "Welfare" captures the substantive, practical sense. LXX's σωτήριον ("salvation/welfare") supports this reading. |

## 4. Yishshaq Kol-Ammi — "Upon Your Mouth" vs. "By Your Word" vs. "Submit to Your Orders" (v. 40)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "upon your mouth all my people shall be directed" | Literal al-pikha |
| LOGOS | "by your word all my people shall be directed" | Formal — mouth → word |
| PNEUMA | "all my people will submit to your orders" | Dynamic |
| **SOPHIA** | **"by your word all my people shall be directed" — LOGOS upheld** | "Upon your mouth" is too literal for English. "Submit to your orders" loses the metaphor. "By your word" preserves the mouth/word connection naturally. The difficult verb nashaq ("to kiss" → "to be directed/governed") is best rendered as "shall be directed." |

## 5. Avrekh — Preserved vs. "Bow the Knee!" (v. 43)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Avrekh" | Preserved untranslated — etymology debated |
| LOGOS | "Avrekh!" | Same — respects narrator's choice |
| PNEUMA | "Bow the knee!" | Hebrew berekh etymology: "bow the knee" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"Avrekh!" preserved — 2-1** | The word has at least three competing etymologies (Egyptian "attention," Hebrew "bow the knee," Akkadian court title). The narrator preserved it as a foreign sound; the LXX rendered it interpretively as κῆρυξ ("herald"). To translate commits to one disputed reading. The narrator's own choice to leave it untranslated should be respected. |

## 6. Liqmatsim — "Handfuls" vs. "Armful" (v. 47)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "by handfuls" | qamats = to grasp with the hand |
| LOGOS | "by handfuls" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "by the armful" | Dynamic rendering for abundance |
| **SOPHIA** | **"by handfuls" — 2-1** | The Hebrew liqmatsim is from qamats ("to close the fist, to grasp"). "Armful" exaggerates beyond the root; "handfuls" is accurate and vivid. |

## 7. Et-Kol-Asher Bahem — "All That Was in Them" vs. "All the Storehouses" (v. 56)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "all that was in them" | Preserves MT's compressed form |
| LOGOS | "all that was in them" | Same — MT followed |
| PNEUMA | "all the storehouses" | Follows LXX's θησαυρούς interpretive expansion |
| **SOPHIA** | **"all that was in them" — 2-1** | The MT's compressed pronominal suffix is deliberately terse; the meaning is clear from context. The LXX's interpretive expansion (θησαυρούς, "storehouses") supplies what the reader can infer. Follow the MT. |

---

*Genesis 41: 7 disagreements logged. SOPHIA ruled with LOGOS on 3 items (vv. 1, 16, 40), with EMET/LOGOS on 4 items (vv. 14, 43, 47, 56). The longest chapter in Genesis (57 verses) produced a moderate number of disagreements — most centered on register and degree of literalness rather than theology. All three translators agreed on the major theological and structural decisions: "magicians" for chartummim, "the spirit of God" for ruach Elohim, "pit" for bor (per established convention), preservation of Zaphenath-paneah and the naming etymologies of Manasseh and Ephraim. PNEUMA's tendency toward dynamic rendering was overruled in several instances where the MT's compression, literalness, or foreign opacity carried interpretive significance.*
